title: 5 hormonal symptoms women should never ignore
description: LIFESTYLE NEWS - Hormonal health is often...
bodyText:
LIFESTYLE NEWS - Hormonal health is often treated as a footnote in women’s healthcare. Symptoms are downplayed, dismissed, or brushed off as stress, age, or “just one of those things.”

But hormone imbalances can have wide-reaching effects on your physical and emotional well-being, and some signs are too serious to ignore, even if you’ve been told they’re nothing to worry about.


> 5 hormonal symptoms to never ignore


Heavy or irregular periodsA disrupted cycle is more than just an inconvenience. If your period is extremely heavy, lasts more than seven days, or vanishes altogether, it may be a sign that something’s off.

Conditions like endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), fibroids, or thyroid dysfunction often manifest in cycle changes.

Bleeding through sanitary products every hour or spotting between cycles isn’t something you should feel you have to endure. Even if you’re approaching perimenopause, consistently heavy periods can signal that your body is working overtime to regulate hormone production—and that’s worth investigating.

Constant fatigueThere’s a big difference between feeling a little run-down and being utterly drained, no matter how much rest you get.


> Persistent fatigue is one of the most common yet often overlooked symptoms of hormonal imbalance. Issues such as low thyroid function (hypothyroidism), cortisol dysregulation due to chronic stress, low iron, or vitamin B12 deficiency could all be at play.


If your energy levels are consistently low and sleep doesn’t seem to make a difference, it’s time to seek a medical opinion and request a full hormonal and nutritional workup.

Stubborn adult acneBreakouts aren’t just a teenage issue. Many women in their 30s, 40s and beyond experience acne, especially along the jawline and chin, classic signs of a hormonal imbalance.

High androgen levels, as seen in PCOS, or fluctuating oestrogen and progesterone during perimenopause, are common triggers.

If topical treatments and skincare routines don’t make a difference, the cause could be internal. Persistent acne is your body’s way of asking for support, not stronger exfoliants.

Unexpected hair lossHair fall is distressing, particularly when it happens in clumps or if you notice your parting widening over time.


> Hormones play a crucial role in regulating hair growth, so changes may indicate an underlying issue such as thyroid dysfunction, low iron levels, or the hormonal shifts linked with menopause.


If you’re experiencing excessive shedding, don’t chalk it up to stress or ageing without exploring the full picture. Your body may be signalling that something is out of balance.

Severe mood swingsMood swings, irritability, anxiety, or a loss of libido are frequently brushed off as “just hormonal”—but that doesn’t mean they aren’t medically significant.

Oestrogen, progesterone and testosterone all affect brain chemistry, mood regulation, and motivation. When those hormones fluctuate significantly, so does your mental well-being.

If you feel completely unlike yourself, experience crushing low moods before your period, or notice your sex drive has disappeared entirely, it’s not something to suffer through silently.

These are valid symptoms of hormone disruption and deserve attention.


> Trust your instincts


Women are often conditioned to downplay their symptoms. But if something feels off in your body, you have every right to ask questions, seek answers, and push for a diagnosis.

These symptoms aren’t just inconveniences—they can be signs of deeper imbalances that, left untreated, may worsen over time.

If your concerns have been brushed aside before, don’t let that stop you from advocating for yourself again. Hormonal health isn’t a luxury—it’s a crucial part of your overall wellbeing.

‘We bring you the latest Garden Route, Hessequa, Karoo news’
